does: This paper introduces RED-EVAL, a red-teaming benchmark that evaluates safety vulnerabilities in large language models using a novel Chain of Utterances (CoU) prompting technique. The authors demonstrate that CoU-based attacks successfully jailbreak closed-source models like GPT-4 and ChatGPT, eliciting harmful responses in over 65% and 73% of cases respectively, and trigger harmful outputs in over 86% of attempts across eight open-source LLMs. Additionally, the paper proposes RED-INSTRUCT, a two-phase safety alignment approach that begins with HARMFULQA, a dataset of 1.9K harmful questions and 9.5K related adversarial examples collected using CoU prompting techniques to improve model robustness against these attack patterns.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research into LLM safety evaluation and adversarial robustness. It extends prior red-teaming efforts by introducing a systematic Chain of Utterances attack methodology, contributing to the literature examining vulnerabilities in instruction-tuned and RLHF-aligned models like GPT-4 and ChatGPT. The paper responds to earlier work on jailbreaking techniques and safety benchmarks by providing a more consistent, scalable evaluation framework. It also connects to research on safety data collection and alignment methods, building upon approaches that use adversarial examples and harmful queries to improve model behavior through targeted training.
enables: This research enables the development of more robust safety alignment techniques by providing both a systematic red-teaming methodology and a labeled adversarial dataset that future work can leverage. The RED-EVAL benchmark establishes a foundation for comparative safety evaluation across models, potentially becoming a standard tool for measuring vulnerability to CoU attacks. The RED-INSTRUCT approach points toward data-driven solutions for safety alignment that specifically address chain-of-thought style jailbreaks. These contributions could accelerate the development of more reliable defense mechanisms and inform best practices for deploying LLMs at scale, while also establishing baselines that motivate further research into adaptive attack and defense strategies.
